posts.title: agents that work vs agents that demo
posts.hook: Most AI agent demos would fall apart if you pointed them at a real CRM with 40,000 dirty records.
posts.body:
Most AI agent demos would fall apart if you pointed them at a real CRM with 40,000 dirty records.

I've been building agentic workflows for GTM teams since early 2025. The pattern I keep seeing: teams buy the agent hype, spin up a LangChain prototype, show it to leadership... then quietly shelve it three weeks later.

The gap isn't the framework. It's the plumbing.

Agents need clean inputs to produce clean outputs. That means your enrichment layer, your scoring logic, your CRM hygiene all have to be solid before you hand the keys to an autonomous system.

What actually works in production right now:

✅ Agents that route leads based on structured enrichment data (not raw LinkedIn scrapes)
✅ Agents that draft sequences from scored intent signals (not vibes)
✅ Agents that flag data quality issues before they compound

What doesn't work:

❌ Agents that "research prospects" by summarizing their About page
❌ Agents that write emails without knowing your ICP scoring
❌ Agents bolted onto a CRM that hasn't been cleaned since 2023

The boring work of data infrastructure is what makes the exciting work of AI agents possible. Every team that skips straight to agents without fixing their data layer ends up back at square one.
posts.cta: What's the first agent workflow that actually stuck in your stack?

remixes.label: autopsy-report
remixes.hook: I've watched six agent projects die the same death this year. The cause is never what leadership thinks.
remixes.body:
I've watched six agent projects die the same death this year. The cause is never what leadership thinks.

Leadership blames the model. Engineering blames the framework. The actual killer? Garbage in, garbage out. Every single time.

Here's the typical timeline. Week 1, someone builds a demo on a curated dataset of 200 clean records. It looks incredible. Week 2, they connect it to the real CRM. 40,000 records with duplicate contacts, missing fields, contradictory lifecycle stages, and phone numbers stored in the company name field.

Week 3, the agent starts hallucinating recommendations based on bad data. Week 4, it's shelved.

The teams that actually ship production agents treat data quality as the first sprint, not a follow-up ticket. They build validation layers before they build agent logic. They write normalization scripts before they write prompts.

I started requiring a data audit before any agent project kicks off. Simple pass/fail on field completeness, deduplication rate, and schema consistency. If the CRM doesn't pass, we fix the data first.

It's not glamorous. Nobody tweets about spending two weeks cleaning Salesforce records. But it's the difference between an agent that runs in production and one that lives in a slide deck.
remixes.cta: Have you ever killed an agent project because of data quality? What broke first?

remixes.label: framework-trap
remixes.hook: LangChain isn't why your agent failed. Your Salesforce instance is.
remixes.body:
LangChain isn't why your agent failed. Your Salesforce instance is.

There's a running joke in AI engineering circles. The framework wars are just a distraction from the fact that most production failures happen before the LLM even gets invoked.

I audit GTM agent implementations for a living. The breakdown looks like this. About 15% of failures are model-related, bad reasoning, hallucinations, context window limits. Around 25% are orchestration issues, retry logic, error handling, timeout management. The remaining 60% are data problems.

Duplicate records that make the agent process the same lead twice. Missing fields that cause null reference errors three steps into a workflow. Inconsistent formatting that turns a phone number lookup into a wild goose chase.

The fix isn't a better framework. It's a better data contract. Define what clean looks like before you write a single agent function. Build validation gates at every input boundary. Monitor data drift the same way you'd monitor model drift.

I've started scoping every agent project as two workstreams. Workstream A is data readiness. Workstream B is agent logic. Workstream B doesn't start until Workstream A passes. It adds time upfront but eliminates the three-week shelving pattern completely.
remixes.cta: What percentage of your agent debugging time is actually data debugging?

Most AI agent demos would fall apart if you pointed them at a real CRM with 40,000 dirty records.

I've been building agentic workflows for GTM teams since early 2025, and here's what I tell every client before we start. Your agent is only as good as the data it reads.

The pattern is predictable. A team gets excited, builds a prototype on clean sample data, demos it to leadership, gets budget. Then they connect it to production systems and everything breaks. Not because the model is bad. Because the CRM has 12,000 duplicate contacts and half the lifecycle stages are wrong.

My recommendation: before you write a single line of agent logic, run a data quality audit. Check field completeness rates. Identify your deduplication backlog. Map out schema inconsistencies across your tools. If your data doesn't pass a basic health check, fix that first.

The teams I've seen succeed follow a simple rule. Data readiness is sprint zero. Agent development is sprint one. Never the other way around.

This isn't optional. It's the difference between a production system and a demo that quietly disappears. Build the plumbing before you build the intelligence layer, and you'll skip the cycle of hype followed by disappointment that I see repeat across the industry every quarter.

But nobody wants to hear that. The AI agent space is running on vibes right now. Founders raising rounds on demo videos. Consultants selling "agent transformations" without ever looking at the client's data model. LinkedIn influencers posting agent workflows that only work on synthetic datasets.

Meanwhile, in the real world, teams are burning quarters trying to make agents work on top of CRMs that have been accumulating garbage data since 2019.

Here's . Most companies aren't ready for agents. Not because the technology isn't there. Because their data hygiene is a disaster. And no amount of prompt engineering fixes a contact record where the phone number is stored in the email field.

The LangChain-to-production pipeline has a 90% failure rate and almost nobody talks about it. They just move on to the next framework and pretend the last attempt didn't happen.

You want agents that actually work? Stop obsessing over which orchestration library to use. Go clean your CRM. Deduplicate your contacts. Standardize your fields. It's boring, unglamorous work. But it's the only work that matters. Everything else is theater.

posts.title: GTM engineer is a real job now
posts.hook: Two years ago if you said "GTM engineer" in a job interview, people would ask if you meant solutions engineer.
posts.body:
Two years ago if you said "GTM engineer" in a job interview, people would ask if you meant solutions engineer.

Now there are dedicated GTM engineering roles at companies doing $20M+ ARR. The function went from "RevOps person who also writes Python" to a distinct discipline with its own stack.

The shift happened because the tooling got too complex for pure ops people and too GTM-specific for pure engineers.

You need someone who understands why a 3-step waterfall enrichment in Clay matters for deliverability. Who can build a custom scoring model in Python but also knows that scoring means nothing if your ICP definition is wrong. Who can set up Instantly warmup pools but also architect the data pipeline feeding them.

The best GTM engineers I've talked to share one trait. They did the manual version first. They sat in the SDR seat, built buying committees by hand, sent hundreds of emails a day. Then they automated what they understood from experience.

That's different from an engineer who reads the API docs and builds what seems logical. The person who's been in the trenches builds for edge cases they've personally hit.

If you're an SDR who's been tinkering with Clay, writing formulas, building automations on the side... you might already be a GTM engineer. You just don't have the title yet.
posts.cta: SDRs turned builders, what was the first thing you automated?

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building a GTM engineering function from scratch, emphasizing the need for specialized engineers who bridge product, sales, and marketing. remixes.label: origin-story
remixes.hook: The first GTM engineer I ever met was an SDR who got tired of manually enriching leads and taught herself Python over a weekend.
remixes.body:
The first GTM engineer I ever met was an SDR who got tired of manually enriching leads and taught herself Python over a weekend.

That was 2024. She built a script that pulled company data from Clearbit, matched it against their ICP scoring model, and auto-routed qualified leads to the right AE. Saved her team about 15 hours a week.

Her manager didn't know what to call the role. "RevOps with coding skills" was the best they could come up with.

Fast forward to today. Companies at $20M+ ARR are hiring dedicated GTM engineers. Not RevOps people who dabble in automation. Not software engineers who happen to work on sales tools. A distinct function that sits between product engineering and revenue operations.

The catalyst was complexity. Modern GTM stacks have 15 to 30 tools generating signals. Someone needs to build the pipelines that connect intent data to outbound sequences to CRM updates to pipeline reporting. That's not a Zapier problem anymore. It's an engineering problem with GTM domain expertise.

The people landing these roles tend to share a background. They started in an operator seat, SDR, AE, RevOps. They hit a ceiling where manual processes couldn't scale. They learned to code to solve their own problems. Now they're building the infrastructure that makes revenue teams run.

The career path didn't exist three years ago. Now it's one of the highest-leverage roles in B2B SaaS.
remixes.cta: If you're making the jump from ops to engineering, what skill was hardest to pick up?

remixes.label: stack-breakdown
remixes.hook: Here's the actual tech stack of a GTM engineer in 2026. It looks nothing like what a traditional software engineer uses.
remixes.body:
Here's the actual tech stack of a GTM engineer in 2026. It looks nothing like what a traditional software engineer uses.

I've been building GTM infrastructure for over a year now. The tooling has matured enough that there's a recognizable stack emerging across companies.

Data layer: enrichment APIs like Apollo, Clearbit, and custom scrapers. A warehouse or lakehouse for signal aggregation, usually Snowflake or BigQuery, sometimes just Supabase if you're lean. Python or TypeScript for ETL pipelines.

Orchestration layer: workflow engines for sequencing multi-step processes. Not Zapier. Real orchestration that handles retries, branching logic, and error states. Temporal, Inngest, or custom-built on top of queues.

Execution layer: CRM integrations, outbound platforms, meeting schedulers, Slack notifications. This is where most GTM engineers spend their debugging time because every tool has a slightly different API contract.

Intelligence layer: LLMs for personalization, lead scoring models, intent signal aggregation. This is the newest piece and still the most unstable.

The people who thrive in this role aren't the best coders. They're the ones who understand the revenue motion deeply enough to know which pipeline to build next. Domain expertise is the moat. The coding is learnable.
remixes.cta: What's the one tool in your GTM stack you'd build in-house if you had the engineering bandwidth?

Now suddenly every B2B company wants one. And most of them have no idea what the role actually is.

The uncomfortable part? Half the job postings I see are just RevOps roles with a fancier title and a Python requirement bolted on. Companies want the output of GTM engineering without actually building the function properly.

Stop posting GTM engineer roles with RevOps compensation. The skill set is engineering-grade. Pay accordingly.

posts.title: shipping alone with AI copilots
posts.hook: Shipped a full client-facing feature last Tuesday. Design, frontend, API, deployment. Total team size: me and Claude.
posts.body:
Shipped a full client-facing feature last Tuesday. Design, frontend, API, deployment. Total team size: me and Claude.

This isn't a flex about working alone. It's an observation about what's changed.

18 months ago that same feature would have required a designer, a frontend dev, and a backend dev coordinating across three sprints. Now a single person with strong context on the problem and good AI tooling can move at a pace that used to require a small team.

The leverage comes from context compression. I know the codebase, the customer need, and the technical constraints. The AI handles the mechanical translation. No handoff meetings. No ticket grooming. No waiting for someone to review my PR at 4pm on a Friday.

But there's a catch nobody talks about. Solo shipping with AI amplifies your judgment, good and bad. If your architectural instincts are solid, you move faster. If they're off, you build the wrong thing faster.

AI copilots don't replace the need for taste. They replace the need for extra hands once you've decided what to build.

The people crushing it as solo builders right now aren't the ones with the best prompts. They're the ones with the deepest domain knowledge and the clearest sense of what to build next.
posts.cta: What's the biggest thing you've shipped solo with AI assistance?

remixes.label: economics-angle
remixes.hook: The cost of shipping a feature dropped by roughly 80% in the last 18 months. Nobody's talking about what that means for team structure.
remixes.body:
The cost of shipping a feature dropped by roughly 80% in the last 18 months. Nobody's talking about what that means for team structure.

I track my own build velocity pretty closely. A client-facing feature that would have taken a three-person team across three sprints in 2024 now takes me about a day and a half with AI assistance.

The math is uncomfortable for anyone who manages engineering headcount. If one person with strong domain context and good AI tooling can ship what used to require three people, the implications ripple through hiring plans, team composition, and project scoping.

But here's the nuance people miss. The bottleneck didn't disappear. It shifted. Typing code was never the hard part. Understanding the problem, making architectural decisions, knowing what to build and what to skip. That's where the real work lives. AI accelerates the execution. It doesn't replace the judgment.

What I've found is that solo shipping with AI amplifies whatever strengths and weaknesses you already have. If you have deep context on the problem space, you move incredibly fast. If you don't, you just produce wrong things faster.

The winning pattern isn't "replace your team with AI." It's "give your strongest builders AI tools and watch what happens to their output." The leverage is multiplicative, not additive. Strong context times strong tooling equals outsized results. Weak context times strong tooling equals fast mistakes.
remixes.cta: Has AI changed how you think about team sizing for new projects?

remixes.label: workflow-reveal
remixes.hook: Here's exactly how I shipped a full feature in one day. No team, no sprints, no standups.
remixes.body:
Here's exactly how I shipped a full feature in one day. No team, no sprints, no standups.

Last Tuesday I built and deployed a client-facing feature. Design through deployment. I want to walk through the actual workflow because I think the specifics matter more than the headline.

Morning: I sketched the component structure and data flow in a markdown doc. Not a design tool. Just text describing what the UI should do and how the API should behave. Fed that to Claude as context.

Mid-morning: Generated the React components and API routes. But here's the key. I wasn't accepting output blindly. Every generated file got reviewed against the architecture I'd already decided on. Probably rewrote 30% of what the AI produced. The other 70% was solid and saved me hours.

Afternoon: Integration testing, edge case handling, and deployment. This part was mostly manual. AI helped with writing test cases but the actual debugging required human judgment about expected behavior.

End of day: Live in production.

What made this possible wasn't just having AI. It was having deep context on the codebase, the client's needs, and the system architecture. The AI handled the mechanical coding. I handled every decision about what to build, how to structure it, and what quality bar to hit.

The people who'll thrive in this era aren't the fastest coders. They're the ones with the clearest thinking about problems.
remixes.cta: Walk me through your solo shipping workflow. What does your AI-assisted build process actually look like?

This isn't about working alone for the sake of it. It's about recognizing a structural shift in how software gets built.

18 months ago that same feature would have required a designer, a frontend dev, and a backend dev coordinating across three sprints. The coordination overhead alone would have added a week.

Here's what I recommend paying attention to if you're leading a team or building solo. The bottleneck has shifted from execution to judgment. AI handles the mechanical parts of coding well enough that the limiting factor is now your understanding of the problem, your architectural taste, and your ability to specify what you want clearly.

This means two things. For individuals, invest heavily in domain expertise and system thinking. Those multiply the value of AI tooling. For organizations, reconsider your team structures. You may not need fewer people, but you likely need them configured differently. Smaller teams with deeper context will consistently outperform larger teams with shallow context.

The practical advice: start tracking your velocity on features where you use AI assistance versus features where you don't. Measure the delta. Then ask what's creating the gap. It's usually context depth and problem clarity, not the specific AI tool.

And before someone says "but that won't work for complex systems at scale," . You're right. This specific workflow doesn't replace a 50-person engineering org building distributed systems.

But it absolutely replaces the three-person team doing a standard feature build. And that's most of the work happening at most companies.

The uncomfortable conversation nobody in engineering leadership wants to have: a significant chunk of feature work at your company could be done by one senior person with AI assistance in a fraction of the time your current team structure requires. Not because your team is bad. Because the coordination overhead of multi-person feature teams is enormous and often unnecessary.

Three sprints of planning, standups, code reviews, and handoffs for something one person with full context could ship in two days. We've been so trained to think "more people equals faster delivery" that we forgot the mythical man-month was written in 1975.

The question isn't whether AI replaces developers. It doesn't. The question is whether your team structure still makes sense when individual output increases by 5x. Most companies haven't seriously asked that question yet. The ones that do will have a significant cost advantage.

posts.title: GPU clusters are someone else's problem
posts.hook: You don't need to understand GPU cluster architecture to build production AI features. That take will upset some people.
posts.body:
You don't need to understand GPU cluster architecture to build production AI features. That take will upset some people.

There's a growing disconnect between AI infrastructure discourse and what application builders actually need to know.

Karpathy posts about training infrastructure and it gets 25K likes. Meanwhile the actual bottleneck for most teams isn't compute. It's getting structured outputs from an API call that don't break their downstream pipeline.

The infrastructure layer matters enormously. For the 200 companies building foundation models. For the other 500,000 companies trying to use AI, the hard problems are:

🔧 Parsing unreliable API responses into your data schema
🔧 Handling rate limits without dropping customer requests
🔧 Versioning prompts so you can actually debug regressions
🔧 Routing between models based on cost and latency requirements

I've spent more time this month debugging JSON parsing from Claude API responses than thinking about anything related to training. That's not because I'm doing it wrong. That's the actual work of building AI into products.

The infrastructure fascination creates a skills gap. Teams hire for ML engineering when they need application engineering with AI literacy. Two very different skill sets.

Learn the API layer deeply. Let the infrastructure teams handle the rest.
posts.cta: Where are you actually spending your time building with AI? Training side or application side?

inspired_by.summary: Explores the infrastructure required for training and deploying large-scale AI models, focusing on GPU clusters and efficient data pipelines. Predicts a surge in demand for specialized AI infra tools amid the agent boom. Calls for open-source contributions to democratize access.

remixes.label: practitioner-lens
remixes.hook: I've spent the last year building AI features for production apps. I have never once needed to think about GPU architecture.
remixes.body:
I've spent the last year building AI features for production apps. I have never once needed to think about GPU architecture.

My daily reality: API calls to Claude, structured output parsing, building evaluation pipelines, designing fallback logic when models return unexpected formats. Practical application engineering.

The discourse on my feed: NVIDIA earnings, training cluster topology, H100 vs H200 benchmarks, debates about whether transformers will be replaced.

These feel like two completely different industries.

The infrastructure layer matters enormously. Someone needs to build and optimize it. But the idea that every AI practitioner needs deep infrastructure knowledge is like saying every web developer needs to understand TCP packet routing.

What I actually need to know: how to get reliable structured output from an LLM. How to build evaluation frameworks that catch regressions. How to design workflows that degrade gracefully when a model call fails. How to manage context windows across complex multi-step operations.

None of that requires understanding how the GPU executes matrix multiplications.

The application layer is where most of the unsolved problems live right now. Reliability, evaluation, user experience, integration with existing systems. These are engineering problems, not research problems. And they're where the majority of AI value creation is happening.

The infrastructure discourse is interesting. But if you're building AI products, your time is better spent mastering the application patterns than following GPU roadmaps.
remixes.cta: Application builders, what's the hardest non-infrastructure problem you're solving right now?

remixes.label: contrarian-flip
remixes.hook: The AI knowledge that actually matters in 2026 has nothing to do with what most AI content covers.
remixes.body:
The AI knowledge that actually matters in 2026 has nothing to do with what most AI content covers.

Scroll through any AI-focused feed. Training breakthroughs, benchmark comparisons, infrastructure debates, research paper summaries. It's fascinating content. It's also almost entirely irrelevant to building AI features that users actually interact with.

The bottleneck for most teams isn't model capability. The models are good enough. The bottleneck is everything around the model. Getting structured data out reliably. Handling edge cases when the model returns something unexpected. Building evaluation pipelines that catch quality regressions before users do. Designing UX patterns that set appropriate expectations.

I talk to AI product teams every week. Their top three problems are never "we need a better model." They're: our output format is inconsistent, we can't measure quality at scale, and users don't trust the results enough to act on them.

These are all application engineering problems. They require the same skills that make someone good at building any complex software system. API design, error handling, testing strategy, user experience research.

The career advice nobody gives: if you want to build AI products, you don't need a machine learning degree. You need strong software engineering fundamentals and the patience to build boring reliability infrastructure around exciting model capabilities.

The glamorous part is the model. The valuable part is everything else.
remixes.cta: What's the most underrated skill in AI product development?

tones.builder:
You don't need to understand GPU cluster architecture to build production AI features. That take will upset some people.

There's a growing disconnect between AI infrastructure discourse and what application builders actually need to know.

Karpathy posts about training infrastructure and it gets 25K likes. Meanwhile the actual bottleneck for most teams isn't compute. It's getting structured outputs from an API call that don't break their downstream pipeline.

The infrastructure layer matters enormously. For the 200 companies building foundation models. For the other 500,000 companies trying to use AI, the hard problems are:

🔧 Parsing unreliable API responses into your data schema
🔧 Handling rate limits without dropping customer requests
🔧 Versioning prompts so you can actually debug regressions
🔧 Routing between models based on cost and latency requirements

I've spent more time this month debugging JSON parsing from Claude API responses than thinking about anything related to training. That's not because I'm doing it wrong. That's the actual work of building AI into products.

The infrastructure fascination creates a skills gap. Teams hire for ML engineering when they need application engineering with AI literacy. Two very different skill sets.

Learn the API layer deeply. Let the infrastructure teams handle the rest.
tones.advisor:
You don't need to understand GPU cluster architecture to build production AI features. That take will upset some people.

But I see working with teams building AI products. The actual bottleneck is almost never compute or model capability. It's the application layer.

Here's what I recommend focusing your learning time on if you're building AI features. First, structured output reliability. Getting consistent, parseable responses from LLMs is harder than it sounds and it's the foundation of every production AI feature. Master this before anything else.

Second, evaluation infrastructure. You need automated ways to catch quality regressions. Manual testing doesn't scale. Build eval pipelines early and run them on every change.

Third, graceful degradation. Models will fail, return unexpected formats, or produce low-quality output. Your system needs to handle all of those cases without breaking the user experience.

Fourth, context management. For any non-trivial AI feature, you're managing conversation history, system prompts, tool results, and user context across multiple interactions. This is a state management problem, not an AI problem.

The infrastructure layer is someone else's problem, and that's fine. The best AI product engineers I work with spend zero time on GPU discourse and all their time on the four areas above. That's where the leverage is for application builders.
tones.provocateur:
You don't need to understand GPU cluster architecture to build production AI features. That take will upset some people.

Good. It should.

The AI space has a gatekeeping problem disguised as intellectual rigor. If you can't explain attention mechanisms from first principles, you're not a "real" AI builder. If you haven't read the latest training infrastructure paper, your opinion doesn't count.

Meanwhile, the people actually shipping AI products that users pay for? Most of them couldn't tell you the difference between an H100 and an H200. They don't need to. They're too busy solving the hard problems that infrastructure discourse completely ignores.

Like getting an LLM to return valid JSON more than 95% of the time. Like building evaluation systems that catch subtle quality degradation. Like designing interfaces where users understand what the AI can and can't do.

Karpathy posts about training infrastructure and gets 25K likes. Someone posts about building a reliable output parsing pipeline and gets 47 likes. Guess which one actually moves the needle for 99% of teams building with AI.

The infrastructure fetish is actively harmful because it convinces capable engineers that they need a PhD before they can contribute. They don't. They need software engineering skills and the willingness to build boring, reliable plumbing around exciting model capabilities. That's the job. And it's where all the value is.

posts.title: outbound is a data engineering problem
posts.hook: The teams cutting outbound volume by 70% while increasing pipeline aren't writing better emails. They're building better data.
posts.body:
The teams cutting outbound volume by 70% while increasing pipeline aren't writing better emails. They're building better data.

I keep seeing the same pattern. Company buys Instantly or Smartlead, writes 47 email variants, A/B tests subject lines for weeks. Reply rates stay flat.

Then someone builds a proper enrichment pipeline and everything changes overnight.

Here's what that actually looks like:

1. Pull ICP companies from your CRM
2. Enrich with technographic + hiring + funding signals in Clay
3. Score each account on 5-8 weighted criteria
4. Only the top 15% make it into a sequence
5. Personalization pulls from the enrichment data, not generic LinkedIn scraping

The email copy barely matters when you're sending to the right person at the right company at the right time with the right context.

One client went from 2% reply rate sending 500 emails/day to 11% reply rate sending 80 emails/day. Same product. Same market. Same SDR writing the emails. The only variable that changed was the data feeding the targeting.

Outbound isn't a copywriting problem. It's a data engineering problem wearing a copywriting costume.

The teams that figure this out build a compounding advantage. Better data means better targeting means better replies means better feedback loops means even better data.
posts.cta: What signal has had the biggest impact on your outbound targeting?

inspired_by.author: Kyle Coleman
inspired_by.summary: Outlines a step-by-step guide to outbound automation using AI tools, reducing manual prospecting by 70%. Covers integration with CRM systems and key metrics for sales ops success. Warns against common pitfalls in scaling automated outreach.

remixes.label: pipeline-blueprint
remixes.hook: I rebuilt a client's outbound pipeline last month. We deleted 80% of their email templates and doubled their reply rate.
remixes.body:
I rebuilt a client's outbound pipeline last month. We deleted 80% of their email templates and doubled their reply rate.

The templates weren't the problem. But they weren't the solution either. The team had been A/B testing subject lines for months with flat results. They had 47 email variants across three sequences. None of it mattered because they were sending to the wrong people.

Here's what we actually changed. We built an enrichment layer that pulls firmographic data, technographic signals, and hiring patterns into a unified scoring model. Before any email gets drafted, every prospect passes through three filters.

Filter one: company fit. Does the firmographic profile match their best customers? Not their ideal customer profile doc, their actual best customers based on retention data.

Filter two: timing signals. Are they hiring for relevant roles? Did they just raise a round? Are they showing intent signals on review sites?

Filter three: contact accuracy. Is this the right person? Is the email verified? Is the phone number current?

Prospects that pass all three filters get personalized outreach. Everyone else gets nothing. That's the 70% volume reduction.

The reply rate doubled because every email went to someone who plausibly needed what the client sold, at a time when they were plausibly in-market. That's not copywriting. That's data engineering.

Stop optimizing the message. Start optimizing who receives it.
remixes.cta: What signals are you using to decide who gets outreach and who doesn't?

remixes.label: signal-hierarchy
remixes.hook: Not all buying signals are created equal. Here's the hierarchy that actually predicts pipeline conversion.
remixes.body:
Not all buying signals are created equal. Here's the hierarchy that actually predicts pipeline conversion.

After building enrichment pipelines for multiple GTM teams, I've noticed a consistent pattern in which signals actually correlate with closed deals versus which ones just generate activity.

Tier 1, highest signal: direct intent. They visited your pricing page, requested a demo from a competitor, or posted on a forum asking for recommendations in your category. These are people actively looking to buy.

Tier 2: organizational change signals. New VP of Sales hired, funding round closed, expansion into new market. These indicate a window where new tool adoption is likely. Not guaranteed, but the timing is right.

Tier 3: technographic fit. They use tools in your ecosystem, their stack has gaps you fill, they're running legacy software you replace. Good for targeting but not for timing.

Tier 4, lowest signal: firmographic match. Right industry, right size, right geography. This is table stakes. Everyone targets by firmographics. There's zero differentiation here.

Most outbound teams build their lists from tier 4 and wonder why response rates are low. The teams generating outsized pipeline work from tier 1 down, not tier 4 up.

The infrastructure required is different for each tier. Firmographic data is cheap and easy. Intent data requires real engineering, custom scrapers, API integrations, signal aggregation, and scoring models.

That's why outbound is becoming a data engineering problem. The competitive advantage isn't the email. It's the signal pipeline feeding the email.
remixes.cta: Which tier of signals drives most of your pipeline? Be honest.

tones.advisor:
The teams cutting outbound volume by 70% while increasing pipeline aren't writing better emails. They're building better data.

I see this pattern repeatedly across the teams I advise. They invest heavily in email copy, A/B testing, and sequence design. Reply rates stay flat. Then someone builds a proper enrichment pipeline and results change overnight.

Here's what I recommend for teams stuck in the copy optimization trap. Step one, audit your targeting. Pull your last 100 closed-won deals and reverse engineer the signals that were present before the deal started. Hiring patterns, funding events, tech stack changes, intent signals. Build your ideal prospect profile from outcomes, not assumptions.

Step two, build an enrichment layer. Aggregate signals from multiple sources into a unified scoring model. Apollo for contacts, job posting scrapers for hiring signals, intent data providers for in-market indicators. The goal is a single score per prospect that reflects likelihood to buy.

Step three, implement a quality gate. No prospect enters a sequence unless they pass your scoring threshold. This is where the volume reduction happens. You're not sending fewer emails because you're lazy. You're sending fewer emails because you're precise.

Step four, personalize based on the signals that qualified them. The enrichment data that got them through the gate becomes the personalization layer. This is where copy actually matters, but only after targeting is right.

The order matters. Data first, targeting second, copy third.
tones.provocateur:
The teams cutting outbound volume by 70% while increasing pipeline aren't writing better emails. They're building better data.

But most outbound teams are still stuck in a copywriting arms race that stopped mattering two years ago.

47 email variants. Weekly A/B tests on subject lines. Debates about whether to use the prospect's first name or company name in the opening line. All while sending to a list that was pulled from a basic firmographic filter that any competitor could replicate in ten minutes.

This is the outbound equivalent of rearranging deck chairs. The email doesn't matter if you're sending it to the wrong person at the wrong time.

I'll say something that will annoy every sales copywriting guru on this platform. A mediocre email to the right prospect at the right time will outperform a perfect email to a random list every single time. It's not even close.

The teams winning at outbound right now look more like data engineering teams than sales teams. They're building enrichment pipelines, intent signal aggregators, and scoring models. They're spending 80% of their effort on who to contact and 20% on what to say.

Every hour your team spends debating email copy instead of building signal infrastructure is an hour wasted. Fix your targeting. The emails will write themselves.

posts.title: developer tools ate GTM
posts.hook: Cursor, Devin, Claude Code. The developer tools conversation is interesting. But the real disruption is what these tools do to go-to-market teams.
posts.body:
Cursor, Devin, Claude Code. The developer tools conversation is interesting. But the real disruption is what these tools do to go-to-market teams.

Think about it. If a single developer can now build and ship what used to take a team of five, that changes:

🔧 How products get built (faster, cheaper)
🔧 Who builds them (smaller teams, more solo builders)
🔧 How they get sold (different buyer, different motion)
🔧 What RevOps looks like (less headcount to manage, more tooling to orchestrate)

I'm watching this play out in real time. Companies that used to need a 10-person dev team now have 3 engineers shipping the same volume. That means their burn rate dropped. Their time to market compressed. Their GTM motion can be leaner.

But most GTM teams haven't adjusted. They're still running playbooks designed for companies with 18-month development cycles and 50-person engineering orgs.

The founders using AI dev tools are moving too fast for traditional sales cycles. By the time your SDR books a demo, they've already built a prototype of the alternative.

GTM teams selling to technical buyers need to understand this new speed. Your value prop has to be about something the buyer genuinely can't build in a weekend with Cursor.

That's a higher bar. And most companies haven't cleared it yet.
posts.cta: How has AI-accelerated development changed the way you sell or buy software?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Highlights emerging developer tools powered by AI for code automation and agentic workflows, such as Cursor and Devin. Argues that these tools will transform software engineering by handling repetitive tasks. Shares examples of productivity gains from early adopters.

remixes.label: buyer-side-disruption
remixes.hook: Every SaaS pricing model assumes your customer has a team. What happens when your buyer is one person with Claude Code and a weekend?
remixes.body:
Every SaaS pricing model assumes your customer has a team. What happens when your buyer is one person with Claude Code and a weekend?

I keep seeing this pattern. A solo founder spins up a tool that replaces a $50k/year platform. Not because they wanted to compete. Because they needed something specific and building it was faster than buying it.

This breaks the traditional GTM playbook in a few ways.

First, your TAM just shifted. The "team of five" that used to evaluate your enterprise product? That team is now two people. Maybe one. They don't need your collaboration features. They don't need your admin console. They need the core thing, and they can build the rest themselves.

Second, the sales cycle collapses. When a technical buyer can prototype their own solution in a day, your demo has to beat what they already built. Not in features. In time-to-value.

Third, content marketing aimed at "decision-making units" misses entirely. The decision maker, the implementer, and the budget holder are the same person now.

The companies adapting fastest are the ones selling to builders, not buying committees. Dev-first GTM isn't a trend. It's the new default for tools under $100k ACV.
remixes.cta: What product categories are most exposed to the "I'll just build it myself" buyer?

remixes.label: death-of-the-sales-team
remixes.hook: The headcount plan your VP of Sales submitted last quarter was already wrong. AI dev tools made it obsolete before the ink dried.
remixes.body:
The headcount plan your VP of Sales submitted last quarter was already wrong. AI dev tools made it obsolete before the ink dried.

Here's the chain reaction nobody's modeling.

Smaller engineering teams ship faster. Faster shipping means shorter release cycles. Shorter cycles mean your sales team is pitching against a moving target. The competitor who had three engineers six months ago now has the same three engineers shipping at the pace of fifteen.

So what breaks?

Outbound sequences that reference feature gaps. Those gaps close in days now, not quarters. Battle cards that compare capabilities. The comparison is stale before your SDR finishes the cadence. ROI calculators built on implementation timelines. Those timelines just got cut by 60%.

The GTM teams that survive this aren't the ones hiring more reps. They're the ones rebuilding their motion around speed. Speed of intel. Speed of content. Speed of technical validation.

I've started telling clients to cut their outbound research cycle from two weeks to two days. If your competitive intelligence is older than a sprint, it's fiction.

The playbook isn't broken. The clock speed changed and nobody updated the assumptions.
remixes.cta: Has your sales team felt the compression yet? What broke first?

The real strategic question here isn't whether AI dev tools are impressive. It's what they do to your GTM assumptions.

When a single developer can build and ship what previously required a team of five, three things change simultaneously.

First, product development economics shift. Faster iteration, lower cost per feature, shorter time to market. This means your competitive moat based on feature breadth erodes faster than your roadmap can defend it.

Second, team composition changes. Smaller teams with higher leverage. This compresses your total addressable market for enterprise tooling and shifts buying patterns toward individual practitioners.

Third, and most critically, the sales motion needs restructuring. The buyer profile is different. Technical decision-makers are consolidating authority. The evaluation process is shorter because the alternative is building it themselves.

My recommendation: audit your GTM assumptions quarterly instead of annually. Map which of your ICPs are getting smaller due to AI tooling. Rebuild your value proposition around the things builders can't easily replicate. Integration ecosystems, data network effects, compliance infrastructure.

The companies that adjust their GTM model to match the new build velocity will outperform those still selling to five-person teams that no longer exist.
tones.provocateur:
Everyone's talking about Cursor and Claude Code like they're developer productivity tools. That's the safe take. The uncomfortable one? These tools are a wrecking ball aimed at your sales org.

Think about what happens when your prospect can build a working version of your product over a weekend. Not a toy. A real, functional version that does the one thing they actually need.

Your demo is cooked. Your "free trial to paid" funnel is cooked. Your entire value proposition of "we built it so you don't have to" is cooked.

And yet. Most GTM leaders I talk to are still running the 2023 playbook. Same ICPs. Same personas. Same assumption that buying is easier than building.

It's not anymore. Building is now easier than sitting through your four-call sales cycle.

The teams that get this are ditching feature-comparison selling entirely. They're selling the things you genuinely cannot build alone. Data moats. Network effects. Regulatory compliance. Everything else is vulnerable.

If your product's main value prop is "it saves your engineering team time," you need to figure out what happens when that team shrinks to one person who doesn't need to save time because the AI does the work.

posts.title: RevOps alignment is a lie
posts.hook: "Align sales ops, marketing ops, and CS ops under one RevOps umbrella." Sounds great on a slide. Falls apart in Salesforce.
posts.body:
"Align sales ops, marketing ops, and CS ops under one RevOps umbrella." Sounds great on a slide. Falls apart in Salesforce.

I tried the unified RevOps approach for a client last year. Merged the data models, standardized the lifecycle stages, built a single dashboard that showed the full customer journey.

It lasted about six weeks before it started rotting.

The problem isn't the vision. It's that each team operates on different timescales with different incentives. Marketing measures in weeks. Sales measures in quarters. CS measures in years. When you force them into one schema, someone's reality gets distorted.

What actually works better: shared data, separate workflows.

Let each team own their operational layer. But pipe everything into a shared enrichment and analytics layer where the handoff points are instrumented. You don't need one dashboard. You need three dashboards that share the same customer ID and the same enrichment data.

The practical version:

✅ Shared contact/account enrichment in Clay
✅ Shared intent signals from your data warehouse
✅ Separate operational tools per team
✅ Instrumented handoff points with SLAs

Unified RevOps works at the data layer. Unified RevOps at the workflow layer creates a political nightmare where every team fights over field definitions.

Build the shared foundation. Let the teams run their own plays on top of it.
posts.cta: Has anyone actually made unified RevOps work at scale? Genuinely curious what the setup looks like.

inspired_by.author: Amy Volas
inspired_by.summary: Explores the evolution of revenue operations in 2024, focusing on aligning sales ops, marketing ops, and customer success. Provides actionable strategies for content strategy to fuel GTM motions. Includes case studies on revops transformations driving 2x pipeline growth.

remixes.label: revops-graveyard
remixes.hook: I've built four "unified RevOps dashboards" for clients. Three of them are dead. Here's what actually killed them.
remixes.body:
I've built four "unified RevOps dashboards" for clients. Three of them are dead. Here's what actually killed them.

It wasn't the technology. Salesforce can handle it. HubSpot can handle it. The data model was sound every time.

It was the humans.

Specifically, it was three people in three departments who each had a different definition of "qualified." Marketing said MQL meant a form fill with a valid email. Sales said it meant a form fill from a target account. CS said it didn't matter because half of them churned anyway.

We standardized the definitions. Put them in a doc. Got sign-off from all three VPs. Beautiful alignment.

Six weeks later, marketing quietly added a new lead score that bypassed the agreed criteria. Sales started moving deals to "Closed Lost" instead of "Disqualified" because it looked better in their pipeline report. CS built a shadow spreadsheet because they didn't trust the dashboard numbers.

The one that survived? The client didn't try to unify everything. They picked three metrics that all three teams would be measured on together. Shared comp, shared number, shared pain. Everything else stayed in departmental silos.

RevOps alignment doesn't fail because of bad architecture. It fails because unified systems create transparency that some stakeholders don't actually want.
remixes.cta: What's the oldest "unified" dashboard in your org? Is anyone still using it?

remixes.label: small-wins-approach
remixes.hook: Stop trying to build one RevOps system. Build three small ones that talk to each other.
remixes.body:
Stop trying to build one RevOps system. Build three small ones that talk to each other.

The unified RevOps dream sounds right. One data model. One lifecycle. One dashboard. Full visibility from first touch to renewal.

But every time I've seen it attempted at scale, the same thing happens. The system gets built. People use it for a month. Then the workarounds start. Shadow spreadsheets. Duplicate fields. "Temporary" custom objects that become permanent.

The issue is organizational, not technical. Each team needs to move at different speeds. Marketing runs weekly experiments. Sales operates on quarterly cycles. CS tracks annual health scores. Forcing them into one rigid model creates friction that eventually breaks the system.

What I've seen work instead: federated ops.

Each team owns their own operational layer. Their own fields, their own automations, their own reports. But there's a thin integration layer that syncs the five or six data points that actually matter across teams. Account status. Revenue. Lifecycle stage. NPS. Engagement tier.

This approach is less elegant on paper. But it survives contact with reality because each team can iterate independently without breaking the shared model.

The best RevOps architecture isn't the most unified one. It's the one that's still accurate six months after launch.
remixes.cta: Are you building one big system or a few connected ones? Which approach has lasted longer?

tones.builder:
"Align sales ops, marketing ops, and CS ops under one RevOps umbrella." Sounds great on a slide. Falls apart in Salesforce.

I tried the unified RevOps approach for a client last year. Merged the data models, standardized the lifecycle stages, built a single dashboard that showed the full customer journey.

It lasted about six weeks before it started rotting.

The problem isn't the vision. It's that each team operates on different timescales with different incentives. Marketing measures in weeks. Sales measures in quarters. CS measures in years. When you force them into one schema, someone's reality gets distorted.

What actually works better: shared data, separate workflows.

Let each team own their operational layer. But pipe everything into a shared enrichment and analytics layer where the handoff points are instrumented. You don't need one dashboard. You need three dashboards that share the same customer ID and the same enrichment data.

The practical version:

✅ Shared contact/account enrichment in Clay
✅ Shared intent signals from your data warehouse
✅ Separate operational tools per team
✅ Instrumented handoff points with SLAs

Unified RevOps works at the data layer. Unified RevOps at the workflow layer creates a political nightmare where every team fights over field definitions.

Build the shared foundation. Let the teams run their own plays on top of it.
tones.advisor:
The unified RevOps model is theoretically sound and practically fragile. Here's why, and what I'd recommend instead.

Merging sales ops, marketing ops, and CS ops under one umbrella requires three things to be true simultaneously. Shared definitions across all teams. Consistent data hygiene from every contributor. And organizational incentives that reward transparency over departmental optimization.

In my experience, you get maybe one of those three. On a good day, two.

The technical build is usually fine. You can standardize lifecycle stages, merge data models, and create unified reporting. The problem surfaces around week four to six, when individual teams start modifying the shared model to serve their own reporting needs.

What I recommend instead: identify the three to five metrics that genuinely require cross-functional alignment. Pipeline velocity. Win rate by source. Net revenue retention. Build your unified layer around only those metrics.

Everything else stays departmental. Marketing owns their attribution model. Sales owns their pipeline stages. CS owns their health scores. You connect them at the seams with lightweight integrations, not a monolithic data model.

This approach is less impressive in a board presentation. But it's the one that still works in Q3 when the system you built in Q1 would have already started rotting.
tones.provocateur:
RevOps alignment is the consulting world's favorite fiction. Everyone sells it. Nobody maintains it.

I've done the unified build. Merged the data models. Got three VPs to agree on lifecycle definitions in a room. Built the dashboard. It looked incredible in the QBR.

It was a zombie within two months.

You know why? Because alignment means visibility. And visibility means accountability. And accountability means your VP of Marketing can't quietly inflate lead numbers anymore. Your VP of Sales can't hide stalled deals in "discovery" for three months. Your CS team can't pretend churn is a "market condition."

The dirty secret of RevOps is that most organizations don't actually want the transparency it creates. They want the slide that says they have it. They want the consultant to come in, build the thing, and leave before it falls apart.

The orgs where I've seen it work all had one thing in common. Not better tooling. Not cleaner data. Shared compensation. When the CMO's bonus depends on the same number as the CRO's bonus, suddenly everyone cares about data accuracy.

If your teams aren't comped on the same metrics, your RevOps initiative is a reporting project with a fancy name.

I need to be honest about something. I've sold the unified RevOps vision to clients. And I've watched it fall apart more often than it's stuck.

posts.title: content that feeds outbound
posts.hook: Your content strategy and your outbound motion should be the same pipeline. Most teams run them as separate functions and wonder why neither converts.
posts.body:
Your content strategy and your outbound motion should be the same pipeline. Most teams run them as separate functions and wonder why neither converts.

Here's how I've seen this work well:

You publish a tactical piece of content. A breakdown of how you solved a specific problem with specific tools. Not thought leadership. Not a whitepaper. Something a practitioner would actually bookmark.

Then you watch who engages. Who comments. Who shares it in Slack channels. Those signals feed directly into your outbound targeting.

The outbound email doesn't say "saw you liked my post." That's cringe. It references the same problem the content addressed and offers something the post didn't cover. A deeper breakdown. A template. A 15-minute walkthrough.

The content built trust before the outbound landed. The outbound delivered value the content only hinted at.

One team I worked with ran this loop for 90 days:

📝 Published 2 tactical posts per week
📊 Tracked engagement signals in Clay
🎯 Fed engaged accounts into a warm outbound sequence
📈 Saw 3.2x higher reply rate on warm vs. cold

Content isn't a brand play. It's a demand gen play when you instrument it correctly.

The gap for most teams: they have content people and outbound people but nobody connecting the signals between them.
posts.cta: Are your content and outbound teams sharing data, or running parallel?

inspired_by.author: Dan Disney
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down content strategy for B2B sales teams, integrating it with outbound automation for higher conversion rates. Shares templates and examples for sales enablement content. Emphasizes data-driven iteration in revops contexts.

remixes.label: signal-based-content
remixes.hook: What if every blog post you published was also a targeting list? That's not a hypothetical. I've been running this exact motion for six months.
remixes.body:
What if every blog post you published was also a targeting list? That's not a hypothetical. I've been running this exact motion for six months.

The setup is simple. Write content about specific technical problems. Track who reads it, how far they scroll, and whether they come back. Then feed those signals directly into your outbound workflow.

Here's what changes.

Your SDR doesn't open with "Hi, I noticed you're in the fintech space." They open with "Saw you spent 8 minutes on our piece about webhook reliability. We built the thing that post describes. Want to see it?"

That's not personalization theater. That's actually relevant context.

The content itself needs to work differently too. Thought leadership pieces generate impressions. Technical breakdowns generate intent signals. If someone reads your 2,000-word post on migrating from REST to GraphQL, that tells you something specific about what they're working on right now.

The implementation: we use a CMS with reader analytics piped into Clay. Clay enriches the reader's company and role, scores the signal, and pushes qualified readers into an outbound sequence in Lemlist. Total lag from content consumption to first outreach: under 24 hours.

The reply rates on these sequences are 3x our cold outbound. Because the prospect already told us what they care about. We just listened.
remixes.cta: Are you tracking content engagement at the individual level, or just aggregate pageviews?

remixes.label: content-ops-teardown
remixes.hook: Your content team publishes on Tuesday. Your outbound team prospects on Thursday. They never talk to each other. This is the most expensive gap in your GTM.
remixes.body:
Your content team publishes on Tuesday. Your outbound team prospects on Thursday. They never talk to each other. This is the most expensive gap in your GTM.

I audited a B2B SaaS company's pipeline last quarter. Their content was solid. Their outbound was competent. But the two teams shared exactly zero data.

Content published case studies about migration challenges. Outbound was targeting accounts going through migrations. Neither team knew what the other was doing.

We wired them together in three steps.

Step one: content topics come from outbound objections. Every "not interested" reply gets categorized. The top three objections become next month's content calendar. Now your content addresses real sales friction, not imagined pain points.

Step two: content engagement feeds outbound targeting. Accounts that consume content go into a priority queue. Outbound sequences reference the specific content they read.

Step three: outbound responses feed content iteration. When a prospect replies to an outbound email saying "we actually solved this with X approach," that becomes a data point for the content team. Real practitioner feedback, not guesswork.

Within 90 days, the company's outbound reply rate doubled and their content organic traffic jumped 40%. Not because either team got better individually. Because they stopped operating as strangers.
remixes.cta: Pull up your content calendar and your outbound sequences side by side. How much overlap do you see?

tones.advisor:
If I'm advising a team on demand gen strategy, the first question I ask is whether their content function and their outbound function share a data layer. In most cases, they don't.

This is the single highest-leverage fix in most B2B GTM motions. Here's the framework.

Content should serve two purposes simultaneously. First, attract organic traffic and build authority. Second, generate intent signals that feed directly into outbound targeting. If your content only does the first job, you're leaving pipeline on the table.

The implementation I recommend starts with topic alignment. Your content calendar should be informed by outbound objections and prospect questions. Not keyword research alone. Not executive brainstorms. Actual friction from sales conversations.

From there, build a signal layer. Track who engages with content at the company and individual level. Pipe those signals into your outbound tool. Whether that's Clay, Apollo, or a custom integration, the point is that your SDR team sees content engagement before they send the first email.

Finally, close the loop. Outbound replies and objections feed back into content planning. This creates a flywheel where each function makes the other more effective.

The teams running content and outbound as separate functions are paying two teams to solve the same problem independently. Merge the data, not the teams.
tones.provocateur:
Your content team is burning budget creating assets that your outbound team has never seen. And your outbound team is writing cold emails that address problems your content already solved. This is somehow considered normal.

Let me put a number on it. If your average blog post costs $500 to produce and your SDR team sends 1,000 emails a month without referencing any of that content, you're paying twice to reach the same audience with disconnected messages.

The real problem is incentive design. Content teams are measured on traffic and engagement. Outbound teams are measured on meetings booked. There's no shared metric, so there's no shared strategy.

I've watched marketing teams celebrate a blog post that got 10,000 views while the sales team booked zero meetings from accounts that read it. Because nobody connected the dots. Nobody even tried.

The fix isn't a monthly "alignment meeting" where both teams nod politely and go back to their silos. It's infrastructure. Content engagement data piped into CRM records. Outbound sequences that dynamically reference content the prospect already consumed. Shared reporting on content-assisted pipeline.

If your content and outbound teams can't answer the question "which accounts are reading our stuff right now," you don't have a demand gen strategy. You have two separate departments spending money near each other.

Quick question for you. Does your outbound team know what your content team published this week? Does your content team know what objections your SDRs are hearing?

If the answer is no to either, you have the most common (and most expensive) gap in B2B go-to-market. Two teams solving the same problem in parallel without sharing notes.

I've been thinking about this a lot because I just helped a client wire these two functions together, and the results were honestly surprising even to me. I want to walk you through the exact setup this week, because it's simpler than you'd expect and the impact is immediate.

posts.title: the build in public trap
posts.hook: Building in public has a failure mode nobody warns you about. You start optimizing for the narrative instead of the product.
posts.body:
Building in public has a failure mode nobody warns you about. You start optimizing for the narrative instead of the product.

I've caught myself doing this. Spending 30 minutes crafting a post about a feature I built in 20 minutes. Choosing which feature to build next based on what would make a better update. Checking engagement metrics when I should be checking error logs.

The best builders sharing publicly right now (Levels, Sahil, the indie hacker crowd) have a rhythm that works: ship first, share second. The sharing is a byproduct of the building, not the other way around.

When it flips and the building becomes a byproduct of the sharing, you're running a content operation disguised as a product company.

My rules for keeping it honest:

🔧 Never post about something I haven't shipped to production
🔧 Share the failures and broken things, not just the wins
🔧 Revenue numbers only when they teach something, not when they flex
🔧 Build log updates come from git history, not from what sounds impressive

The irony of this post isn't lost on me. But the pattern matters. The builders who sustain momentum are the ones who'd keep building even if nobody was watching.

Public accountability is a tool. Don't let it become the product.
posts.cta: How do you keep the building ahead of the sharing?

inspired_by.author: Sahil Lavingia
inspired_by.summary: Sahil reflects on shipping new features to Gumroad while maintaining transparency with his audience. He discusses pivoting from VC-backed to bootstrapped model and the freedom it brings for solo-like building. Shares metrics on recent product updates.

remixes.label: metrics-trap
remixes.hook: I deleted my analytics dashboard from my phone last month. My shipping velocity doubled.
remixes.body:
I deleted my analytics dashboard from my phone last month. My shipping velocity doubled.

I noticed a pattern. Every morning I'd wake up, check post engagement, and let that number set the tone for the day. Good numbers meant I felt motivated. Bad numbers meant I'd spend the first hour tweaking my content strategy instead of writing code.

The content was running the product. Not the other way around.

This is the part of building in public nobody talks about. The feedback loop is addictive because it's immediate. Ship a feature, post about it, get likes within minutes. That dopamine cycle is faster than the actual product feedback cycle, which takes weeks or months.

So your brain starts optimizing for the fast loop. You build features that photograph well. You write code that makes good screenshots. You pick the task that has a better story over the task that has a bigger impact.

I've started using a simple rule. Build for three days, share on the fourth. No posting about work in progress. No "just shipped" updates until I've had 72 hours to see if the thing actually works in production.

The constraint kills the performative building. What's left is work worth sharing because it actually survived contact with users, not just followers.

The irony of this post is not lost on me.
remixes.cta: What's your ratio of building time to sharing time? Be honest.

remixes.label: audience-capture-angle
remixes.hook: Building in public is audience capture wearing a hard hat. You start as a builder with an audience. You end as a content creator with a side project.
remixes.body:
Building in public is audience capture wearing a hard hat. You start as a builder with an audience. You end as a content creator with a side project.

I've watched this happen to people I respect. Talented developers who started sharing their journey and slowly, almost imperceptibly, became full-time narrators of work they barely have time to do anymore.

The economics make it rational. A tweet about your product gets 50 impressions. A tweet about your revenue gets 5,000. A tweet about your failures gets 50,000. The algorithm rewards the story about the building, not the building itself.

So you start curating which problems to solve based on narrative potential. You pick the feature that has a better before/after screenshot. You delay a boring but critical infrastructure fix because there's nothing to post about.

The worst version of this is when your audience starts telling you what to build. They don't use your product. They follow your journey. Their feature requests optimize for entertainment, not utility.

The builders I admire most share quarterly, not daily. They disappear for weeks and come back with something substantial. They treat their audience as a distribution channel, not a feedback mechanism.

Building in public works best when the public part is a byproduct. The moment it becomes the primary output, you've been captured.
remixes.cta: When was the last time you built something without telling anyone about it?

tones.advisor:
Building in public is a legitimate distribution strategy. But it comes with a specific failure mode that I see frequently, and it's worth addressing directly.

The risk is narrative optimization. When your audience feedback loop is faster than your product feedback loop, your decision-making starts drifting toward content performance rather than product performance.

This shows up in subtle ways. Choosing which feature to build based on what makes a better update post. Spending more time on the writeup than the implementation. Checking engagement metrics during time blocked for deep work.

My recommendation for anyone building in public: establish a clear separation between building time and sharing time. Batch your content creation. Don't post about features until they've been live for at least a few days. And track your ratio of building hours to content hours weekly.

If that ratio starts shifting toward content, you've moved from building in public to performing in public. The distinction matters because one creates lasting value and the other creates a following that evaporates the moment you stop posting.

The most effective build-in-public practitioners I've worked with treat their updates as a structured changelog, not a daily diary. Less frequent, more substantial, and always anchored to real user outcomes rather than build aesthetics.
tones.provocateur:
Building in public is just content marketing that's convinced itself it's something nobler.

Here's the test. Take your last ten "build in public" posts and ask: did each of these help my users, or did they help my follower count? If it's mostly the second, you're not building in public. You're performing in public.

I've done this to myself. Spent 30 minutes writing about a feature I built in 20. Chose the photogenic bug fix over the important one. Designed a UI change specifically because it would look good in a before/after screenshot.

At some point I realized my product roadmap was being influenced by what would perform well on LinkedIn. That's not building in public. That's audience capture with a deployment pipeline.

 that the most important work is boring. Database migrations don't get likes. Fixing a race condition doesn't make a good screenshot. Rewriting your auth flow for the third time isn't a compelling narrative arc.

But those are the things that make products actually work.

The builders I respect most are the ones you don't hear from for months. Then they show up with something real. No daily updates. No engagement farming. Just a thing that works, shared when it's ready.

If your build-in-public habit makes you a better builder, keep going. If it makes you a better poster, that's a different career path.

posts.title: the ops-to-engineering pipeline
posts.hook: Watched three RevOps people become GTM engineers this year. None of them planned it. They just kept automating until the job title stopped fitting.
posts.body:
Watched three RevOps people become GTM engineers this year. None of them planned it. They just kept automating until the job title stopped fitting.

The progression looks the same every time:

Phase 1: They learn Clay because manual enrichment is killing them
Phase 2: They connect Clay to their CRM with Zapier or Make
Phase 3: Zapier isn't flexible enough so they learn a bit of Python
Phase 4: They build a custom scoring model because the out-of-box tools don't fit their ICP
Phase 5: Someone asks "wait, are you an engineer now?"

The transition from ops to engineering isn't a career pivot. It's a natural evolution that happens when you care enough about the outcome to learn whatever tool gets you there.

The gap in most orgs: RevOps maxes out at the no-code layer and engineering doesn't understand the GTM context. The person who bridges that gap becomes the most valuable person on the team.

You don't need a CS degree. You need enough technical skill to turn your operational knowledge into automated systems. The operational knowledge is the hard part. The technical skill is learnable.

If you're in RevOps and you keep bumping into the ceiling of what your tools can do... that frustration is data. It's telling you the next version of your career is on the other side of learning to build.
posts.cta: RevOps people who crossed into engineering, what was the trigger that pushed you over?

inspired_by.author: Aasish Pappu
inspired_by.summary: Details the role of GTM engineering in modern SaaS, including custom automation for outbound and sales ops workflows. Advocates for engineering-led revenue operations to outpace competitors. References open-source tools and internal playbooks.

remixes.label: accidental-engineer
remixes.hook: Nobody goes to school for GTM engineering. You end up there because you automated yourself out of your last job title.
remixes.body:
Nobody goes to school for GTM engineering. You end up there because you automated yourself out of your last job title.

The path is almost always the same. You're in RevOps or marketing ops. You're doing manual work that feels like it should be automated. So you learn one tool. Then another. Then you write your first script because the tools aren't flexible enough.

Suddenly you're not really doing ops anymore. You're building systems.

I talked to a marketing ops manager last month who casually mentioned she'd built a Python pipeline that enriches leads, scores them against ICP criteria, and routes them to the right sales rep. All running on a cron job she set up herself.

Her title still says "Marketing Operations Manager." Her actual job is engineering a go-to-market system.

The gap in the market is that nobody is training for this role intentionally. There's no GTM engineering bootcamp. No certification. No clear career ladder. People just drift into it because the problems they're solving keep getting more technical.

The opportunity for anyone in ops right now: lean into the technical side deliberately. Don't wait for the job title to catch up. Learn Python. Learn APIs. Learn how to build the integrations you're currently duct-taping together with Zapier.

The companies hiring for this role are paying 30-50% more than traditional ops roles. And the supply of people who understand both GTM strategy and code is tiny.
remixes.cta: What was the first thing you automated that made you think "wait, am I an engineer now?"

remixes.label: the-tools-ladder
remixes.hook: There's a ladder hidden inside your RevOps tech stack. Spreadsheet to no-code to low-code to code. Most people don't notice they're climbing it until they're three rungs up.
remixes.body:
There's a ladder hidden inside your RevOps tech stack. Spreadsheet to no-code to low-code to code. Most people don't notice they're climbing it until they're three rungs up.

Rung one. You're exporting CSVs from Salesforce and doing VLOOKUPs in Google Sheets. Painful but functional.

Rung two. You discover Clay or Zapier. Now you're building workflows that replace the spreadsheet gymnastics. You feel powerful. This lasts about six months.

Rung three. You hit the ceiling of no-code tools. A workflow needs conditional logic that Zapier can't handle. You find yourself reading Stack Overflow answers about API calls. You paste your first Python snippet into a script and run it. It works. Something shifts.

Rung four. You're writing integrations directly. You've got a GitHub repo. Maybe a cron job. The Zapier subscription you're paying $300/month for could be replaced by 40 lines of Python. So you replace it.

Rung five. You look at your calendar and realize you haven't opened Salesforce in two weeks. You've been building the system that feeds Salesforce. Your pull requests get more attention than your pipeline reports.

Each rung feels like a small step. But the distance between "I export CSVs" and "I maintain a production pipeline" is a career change. The people making this transition right now are landing roles that didn't exist two years ago at salaries that reflect the scarcity.

The ladder is there whether you climb it intentionally or not.
