tones.builder:
Watched three RevOps people become GTM engineers this year. None of them planned it. They just kept automating until the job title stopped fitting.

The progression looks the same every time:

Phase 1: They learn Clay because manual enrichment is killing them
Phase 2: They connect Clay to their CRM with Zapier or Make
Phase 3: Zapier isn't flexible enough so they learn a bit of Python
Phase 4: They build a custom scoring model because the out-of-box tools don't fit their ICP
Phase 5: Someone asks "wait, are you an engineer now?"

The transition from ops to engineering isn't a career pivot. It's a natural evolution that happens when you care enough about the outcome to learn whatever tool gets you there.

The gap in most orgs: RevOps maxes out at the no-code layer and engineering doesn't understand the GTM context. The person who bridges that gap becomes the most valuable person on the team.

You don't need a CS degree. You need enough technical skill to turn your operational knowledge into automated systems. The operational knowledge is the hard part. The technical skill is learnable.

If you're in RevOps and you keep bumping into the ceiling of what your tools can do... that frustration is data. It's telling you the next version of your career is on the other side of learning to build.
tones.advisor:
There's a meaningful career transition happening in B2B operations that I think is underappreciated. Operations professionals are evolving into GTM engineers, and the ones who recognize this early have a significant advantage.

The progression I've observed follows a consistent pattern. It starts with process frustration. Manual enrichment, repetitive data entry, brittle integrations. The ops professional learns a tool like Clay to solve the immediate pain. Then they connect it to their CRM with Zapier or Make.

The pivotal moment comes when off-the-shelf automation tools hit their limits. The person learns basic Python, not from a course, but because they need a specific integration that no-code can't handle. Within months, they're maintaining scripts, managing API connections, and building systems that look a lot like software engineering.

My advice for anyone in this trajectory: be intentional about it. The demand for people who understand both GTM strategy and technical implementation is growing faster than the supply. Companies need people who can design a lead scoring model and also build the pipeline that executes it.

Invest in Python fundamentals, API literacy, and basic data engineering concepts. These skills compound quickly when paired with ops domain knowledge. And start calling yourself a GTM engineer before someone else does. The title shift opens doors to roles paying 30-50% above traditional ops compensation.
tones.provocateur:
RevOps is a career dead end and the smartest people in it already know.

That sounds harsh, but look at what's actually happening. The best RevOps people aren't staying in RevOps. They're becoming engineers. Not because they wanted to. Because the tools they needed didn't exist, so they built them.

The uncomfortable implication: if the best path out of ops is learning to code, maybe the ops role itself is a symptom of insufficient tooling. Every Zapier workflow is a band-aid over a missing feature. Every manual enrichment process is an API call someone hasn't written yet.

The RevOps people making six figures to manage Salesforce workflows are going to have a rough few years. The ones making six figures because they can write the code that replaces Salesforce workflows are going to do very well.

Here's what I find telling. Three RevOps people I know became GTM engineers this year. All three took pay raises. All three said the same thing. They didn't decide to become engineers. They just kept solving problems until the tools stopped being enough.

The question isn't whether ops roles are going away. They're not. But the floor is rising fast. "I know how to configure Salesforce" is becoming table stakes. "I can build the system that feeds Salesforce" is where the leverage is.

If you're in ops and you haven't written your first script yet, the clock is ticking.

Three people in my network made the same transition this year. They started in RevOps or marketing ops. They learned automation tools to save time. Then they learned just enough code to do what the tools couldn't. And somewhere along the way, without planning it, they became engineers.

Not software engineers building consumer apps. GTM engineers building the systems that power sales and marketing pipelines.

I want to break down exactly how this transition happens, because I think a lot of people in ops are already partway there without realizing it. And the compensation difference is significant enough to be worth paying attention to.
